Title: Genomics and biochemical analyses reveal a metabolon key to β-L-ODAP biosynthesis in Lathyrus sativus

Common_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: BAHD acyltransferase superfamily; chloramphenicol acetyltransferase-like domains; intron-less 1320 bp gene
Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Highly expressed in grass pea tissues based on transcript abundance heatmap
Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: In heterologous N. benthamiana leaves, transient expression enabled β-L-ODAP formation when L-DAP was supplied
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Class: non-protein amino acid / neurotoxic specialized metabolite
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Associated with neurolathyrism in humans; may provide an alternative route for oxalate removal in grass pea
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Enhances and confers regioselective β-L-ODAP production by interacting with LsAAE3; transient expression alone in N. benthamiana plus L-DAP is sufficient to generate β-L-ODAP likely using endogenous NbAAE3
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Among candidate BAHD acyltransferases, only BAHD3 produced β-L-ODAP in N. benthamiana and was named LsBOS. 2. Purified LsBOS alone did not convert oxalyl-CoA and L-DAP to β-L-ODAP, arguing against a canonical BAHD acyltransferase mechanism. 3. LsBOS physically interacts with LsAAE3 and strongly enhances β-L-ODAP production while eliminating α-L-ODAP formation. 4. LsBOS likely functions as part of a metabolon that provides regioselectivity to the LsAAE3-catalyzed reaction.
Common_Genes.Interacting_Proteins: LsAAE3; endogenous NbAAE3 inferred in Nicotiana benthamiana
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: candidate gene phylogeny and expression selection; transient heterologous expression in Nicotiana benthamiana; recombinant protein purification; in vitro coupled enzyme assays; LC-MS metabolite profiling; SPR; co-immunoprecipitation
Common_Genes.Omics_Data: Genome assembly and annotation; RNA-seq; ENA: PRJEB33571; GEO: GSE223956; Zenodo: 10.5281/zenodo.7390878
Common_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: L-DAP co-infiltration was required for β-L-ODAP formation in N. benthamiana; reaction outcome depends on pH and CoA/CoA analog presence in coupled assays
Common_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Knocking out LsBOS may lower β-L-ODAP, but residual β-L-ODAP may still form through LsAAE3 alone; complete elimination may be difficult
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Primary target for gene editing or TILLING to reduce β-L-ODAP because it appears to be the main dedicated terminal-step specificity factor and highly expressed in tissues
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Authors conclude LsBOS has a non-canonical BAHD role analogous to Arabidopsis EPS1: it does not act as a standard oxalyl-CoA-dependent acyltransferase and likely operates through close interaction with LsAAE3 in a metabolon.

Pathway_Genes.Enzyme_Name: acyl-activating enzyme 3 / oxalyl adenylase / oxalyl-CoA synthetase-like enzyme
Pathway_Genes.Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway: final step of β-L-ODAP formation linking oxalate metabolism to ODAP biosynthesis
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: directly forms target product in concert with LsBOS; also forms precursor-like activated intermediate
Pathway_Genes.Primary_Substrate: oxalate; ATP; L-DAP; CoA/CoA as activator depending on reaction mode
Pathway_Genes.Primary_Product: β-L-ODAP; α-L-ODAP in vitro without LsBOS; oxalyl-CoA under low-pH ligase conditions
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Activates oxalate using ATP to form an oxalyl-AMP intermediate and transfers the oxalyl group to L-DAP to produce ODAP; in an alternative canonical reaction forms oxalyl-CoA from oxalate and CoA, but this is favored only at low pH.
Pathway_Genes.Subcellular_Localization: Cytoplasm (inferred by authors from AAE3 localization in other plants)
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Expressed in grass pea tissues based on RNA-seq heatmap of genes of interest
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Expressed in grass pea tissues; transcript abundance assessed across multiple tissues
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Class: non-protein amino acid / neurotoxic specialized metabolite
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Associated with neurolathyrism in humans; may function in oxalate removal and defense-related metabolism in grass pea
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Promotes β-L-ODAP biosynthesis; alone produces small amounts of both β-L-ODAP and α-L-ODAP in vitro, while with LsBOS drives strong and regioselective β-L-ODAP production
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. LsAAE3 links oxalate catabolism to β-L-ODAP biosynthesis. 2. LsAAE3 alone can synthesize oxalyl-CoA at pH 6.0, but at pH 8.0 it instead produces small amounts of β-L-ODAP and α-L-ODAP from oxalate, ATP and L-DAP. 3. In combination with LsBOS, LsAAE3 catalyzes greatly enhanced and regioselective β-L-ODAP formation. 4. CoA acts mainly as an activator in the β-L-ODAP-forming reaction, supported by nanomolar apparent Km and stimulation by non-reactive CoA analogs in the coupled system.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: heterologous expression; recombinant protein purification; in vitro enzyme assays; LC-MS metabolite profiling; kinetic analysis; pH optimum assays; SPR; co-immunoprecipitation
Pathway_Genes.Omics_Data: Genome assembly and annotation; RNA-seq; ENA: PRJEB33571; GEO: GSE223956; Zenodo: 10.5281/zenodo.7390878
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Reaction pH strongly affects activity; low pH favors oxalyl-CoA formation, physiological/alkaline pH with L-DAP and CoA favors ODAP formation
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Key control step in terminal β-L-ODAP formation, especially through interaction with LsBOS
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Disrupting LsAAE3 may cause oxalate hyperaccumulation, calcium oxalate crystal buildup, reduced plant health, poorer straw palatability, and increased susceptibility to oxalate-secreting pathogens
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Potential target for lowering β-L-ODAP, but editing requires caution because complete loss may impair oxalate detoxification; authors suggest effective elimination of β-L-ODAP may require restoring higher-pH oxalate catabolic function as well as disrupting LsBOS
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Authors argue against the standard model in which LsBOS simply uses oxalyl-CoA as substrate; instead LsAAE3 is repurposed in grass pea as an adenylase in a metabolon with LsBOS. MtAAE3 can substitute in vitro with LsBOS, but grass pea LsAAE3 shows unusual low-pH optimum for oxalyl-CoA formation.
